Genes.Additional_Notes: Endogenous tomato chi transcript was undetectable in fruit peel at all developmental stages, while chs and fls transcripts were present — identifying CHI as a rate-limiting step in peel flavonol biosynthesis. Accumulation of isoquercitrin in high-expressing lines suggests a downstream rhamnosyl transferase may become rate-limiting. Lycopene concentration was not altered in transgenic fruit. No gross phenotypical differences except slight dullness in red color tone due to depletion of yellow naringenin chalcone. Transgene copy number: 1–2 copies per line.
Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis (key enzymatic step in flavonoid/flavonol biosynthesis pathway)
Genes.Control_Genotype: pSJ89 (binary plasmid with GUS gene under CaMV 35S promoter) and azygous segregants
Genes.Directionality: OE of chi → ↑ CHI enzyme activity → ↓ naringenin chalcone (substrate depleted) → ↑ flavonol end products in fruit peel
Genes.Evidence_Figures_Tables: Fig. 3 (Northern blot: chi transcript undetectable in control peel), Fig. 4A (quercetin levels, up to 66-fold increase), Fig. 4B (kaempferol levels, up to 57-fold increase), Fig. 4C (inverse relationship between quercetin increase and naringenin chalcone depletion), Fig. 5A/B (HPLC chromatograms of nonhydrolyzed peel showing rutin, isoquercitrin, kaempferol rutinoside accumulation in transgenics), Fig. 6 (field trial flavonol levels approaching onion levels)
Genes.Evidence_Type: Genetic perturbation (transgenic overexpression) + metabolite quantification by HPLC (both hydrolyzed and nonhydrolyzed extracts) + Northern blot transcript analysis (Fig. 3, confirming chi transcript absent in control peel but present in transgenic peel) + stable inheritance over 4 generations + segregation with transgene + Southern blot confirmation of transgene insertion + field trial validation (Fig. 6)
Genes.Final_Nutrient_Products: Flavonols — specifically quercetin glycosides (rutin/quercetin 3-rutinoside, isoquercitrin/quercetin 3-glucoside, quercetin 3-trisaccharide) and kaempferol glycosides (kaempferol 3-rutinoside and a putative kaempferol glycoside)
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: Naringenin chalcone (substrate, severely depleted in transgenics); naringenin (product of CHI reaction, intermediate toward flavonols)
Genes.Pathway: Flavonoid biosynthetic pathway (naringenin chalcone → naringenin step)
Genes.Pathway_Step: Catalyzes the isomerization of naringenin chalcone to naringenin, which is the committed step toward downstream flavonol (quercetin and kaempferol glycoside) biosynthesis
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Overexpression (OE); Petunia chi-a cDNA driven by constitutive CaMV double 35S promoter (Pd35S) with nos terminator; binary vector pBBC50
Quantitative_Effects: Up to 78-fold increase in fruit peel flavonols
Quercetin in hydrolyzed peel: up to 16.52 mg/g dry weight, representing 66-fold increase over control (Fig. 4A)
Kaempferol in hydrolyzed peel: up to 2.05 mg/g dry weight, representing 57-fold increase over control (Fig. 4B)
Quercetin to kaempferol ratio approximately 10:1 in high-flavonol lines
Naringenin chalcone severely depleted in high-flavonol transformants (Fig. 4C)
Processed paste from transgenics: up to 1.9 mg flavonols/g dry weight paste, 21-fold increase over azygous control paste
65% of total flavonol content of fresh fruit retained in processed paste
Whole-fruit flavonol levels in best lines approach those of yellow onions (Fig. 6)
No change in flavonoid levels in fruit flesh or leaves of transgenics
Genes.Tissue_Stage: Fruit peel (primary site of flavonol accumulation); measured at fully red ripe stage (15–21 days post-breaker); also tested under open field trial conditions; chi transcript detected at low levels in peel at all developmental stages in transgenics vs. undetectable in control peel

Title: Overexpression of petunia chalcone isomerase in tomato results in fruit containing increased levels of flavonols
